title: Maundy Thursday
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## March 29, 2018 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

March 29, 2018 was Maundy Thursday, the day commemorating Jesus's last supper with his disciples and the institution of the Eucharist. The RCL readings would have been Exodus 12:1-14 (the Passover instructions), Psalm 113, 1 Corinthians 11:23-26 (Paul's account of the last supper), and John 13:1-17, 31b-35 (the foot washing narrative). This fell in Holy Week during the season of Lent, specifically the week leading directly to Good Friday and Easter Sunday (April 1, 2018).

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a relatively stable period denominationally in March 2018, between General Conventions (the next would be July 2018 in Austin, Texas). The Diocese of Colorado was preparing for its annual Diocesan Convention (typically held in May), and Holy Week would have been a major liturgical focus across all parishes. There were no major bishop elections or significant controversies specific to the Rocky Mountain region at this moment.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in early spring with typical late-March weather patterns (potential for snow mixed with rain). The Colorado legislature was in active session (January-May annually), with education funding and healthcare issues dominating local political discussion. The 2018 primary election cycle was ramping up as Colorado prepared for midterm elections in November, with gubernatorial races beginning to take shape.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Trump administration was in turmoil over the firing of Secretary of State Rex Tillerson (March 13), with Mike Pompeo being nominated as his replacement. The Mueller investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 election was actively proceeding, generating constant headlines. Cambridge Analytica's data harvesting scandal involving Facebook had just broken public awareness (March 2018), creating significant national concern about data privacy and social media regulation.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Salisbury poisoning of former Russian spy Sergei Skripal and his daughter (March 4) had escalated into a major UK-Russia diplomatic crisis with expulsions of diplomats and talk of sanctions. Syria's civil war continued with Assad's forces consolidating control, and the humanitarian crisis in the region remained a major international concern. North and South Korea were entering a period of renewed diplomatic engagement, with historic talks being prepared for later in spring 2018.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in late March 2018 was dominated by the #MeToo movement's ongoing reckoning with sexual harassment and assault across industries (now one year into the public phase). The 2018 NCAA basketball tournament (March Madness) was in full swing, a major cultural focus for American sports fans. The general mood was volatile and polarized politically, with the midterm elections looming and intense debate about immigration, healthcare, and democratic institutions. Easter was approaching as a major cultural and religious moment, with secular culture increasingly commercializing spring holiday narratives.
